All reports
AZUGA

Voice of Customer Report

What fleet customers say in their own words, and the language shifts that close the gap.

Prepared by Be Vizual. Presented by Ethan Galloway.
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Fleet customers in this category want three plain things: track my van accurately, score me fairly, and let me leave when I want. Azuga sells a “comprehensive safety platform” while its own buyers write “I just want it to track my van,” and the single loudest complaint across Azuga, Motive, and Verizon Connect alike is contract entrapment. The highest-value language shift is to stop selling the platform and start selling the freedom to leave, then make that freedom true.

Three wants run through every usable quote.

First, accurate tracking they can trust. A pest tech says the record is “decent it does a good job of keeping a record of where I go between each job”1 but flags that the location pin put him at a Walgreens when he was at a Home Depot. A pest control manager checked a truck at the office and the system “says it hasn’t left an area it was at 30 minutes ago.”2

Second, fair scoring. Drivers describe a system that punishes the right reaction. One Samsara operations manager keeps a driver’s score intact only because he can prove the harsh brake came when “a deer ran out in front of the driver.”3 An Azuga technician admits he is “not cussing at it as much when it goes off.”4

Third, the freedom to leave. This want is the loudest. A Trustpilot reviewer warns that if you do not cancel 60 days early “they will automatically screw you.”5 A BBB complainant who cancelled says Azuga “want to charge me all future income they would have realized from me had I stayed with them.”6

The gap between how the company talks and how customers talk is widest at the exit. The brand front door promises flexibility; the lived experience for roughly two-thirds of Azuga’s negative reviewers is a 3-year lock-in, a 60-day auto-renewal window, and surprise early-termination or “unused software” fees.7 The single highest-value shift is to replace “auto-renewal” and “term agreement” with the plain promise customers are begging to find: leave when you want, no buyout, no fine print.

This report draws on public reviews, not first-party customer interviews. No Azuga customer was interviewed for this engagement, so Section 8 makes commissioning a primary-interview program the first recommended move.

0.5 Evidence Base and Recommended Primary Research

Every quote in this report comes from a public review surface: Capterra, Trustpilot, the Better Business Bureau, the Aspire community forum, and Azuga’s own published success stories. The nine interviews conducted for this engagement were all Azuga executives and employees. No Azuga customer, partner, or prospect was interviewed.

The public record is dense and one-directional on the dominant themes, which is why the contract and cancellation findings hold. It is thin on the things only a live conversation surfaces: the moment a buyer first decided to look, the exact words that closed the deal, the emotion behind a near-cancellation, and the difference between what the economic buyer wants and what the driver in the truck wants.

Commission a primary-interview and case-study pass, AI-led custom interviews with current Azuga customers across the service trades, to confirm and sharpen four things this public record can only suggest: the emotional registers (resignation, vindication, relief), the buyer-versus-driver split, the hire timeline from first thought to habit, and the win and loss forces at the decision point. Treat this as the next step, not a caveat.

1. What Customers Actually Say

“They will automatically screw you”: the contract is the complaint

The loudest, most repeated complaint in the category lands at the exit, not on the product. Across roughly 55 reviewed verbatims, contract and cancellation entrapment is the single most prevalent theme, present in about two-thirds of Azuga’s negative reviews and BBB complaints, and echoed for Motive and Verizon Connect.8 This is a category-wide pattern Azuga shares, which is exactly why owning the opposite position is open.

The pattern repeats in the same shapes. A Trustpilot reviewer warns the next buyer flatly:

“BUYER BEWARE, if not cancelled 60 days prior to end of your term contract, they will automatically screw you.”9 Persona: Departing Azuga customer. Stage: Use and Succeed.

A BBB complainant who lost a van to a drunk driver could not stop being billed for the destroyed unit:

“I keep calling and calling them but these goombahs keep charging me for the 3rd unit EVERY MONTH… they are absolutely LIERS AND BULLYS. Using their corporate power to abuse a customer.”10 Persona: Owner-operator, 2-3 vehicle fleet. Stage: Use and Succeed.

Another describes the auto-renewal as a trap that sprang after a missed window:

“Months later, they finally reached out conveniently after their auto-renewal had already processed, locking me into another 3-year [contract]… At no point was I verbally informed of an auto-renewal policy. It was only hidden in the fine print… I feel deceived and taken advantage of, and I’m now stuck in a long-term contract that I actively tried to prevent.”11 Persona: SMB fleet decision-maker. Stage: Use and Succeed.

The exit bill stuns people. One reads the early-termination charge as paying for time never used:

“I cancelled their subscription service as it was not working for me… but they want to charge me all future income they would have realized from me had I stayed with them.”12 Persona: SMB subscriber. Stage: Use and Succeed.

A Day Habilitation provider expected a small penalty and got a four-figure bill with no explanation:

“They never went into detail about the cancellation policy. We figured it would be a simple 1-month penalty, or a couple of months at most… Today, I got an invoice for early cancellation of the service, and they billed me $4,500. They don’t explain why it’s that much.”13 Persona: Small service business owner. Stage: Purchase.

The same trap is why prospects walk before they buy. A landscaping operator on the Aspire forum compared his month-to-month terms with Azuga’s:

“Our contract is also month to month where as Azugas is a 3 year contract. I thought that was alarming… We may have even given it a shot if it was not for the three year contract.”14 Persona: Service-trades operator evaluating Azuga. Stage: Evaluation.

The competitors are no cleaner, which is the opening. A Motive reviewer: “They will unethically lock you in a 48 month contract even when they tell you that it’s not a contract.”15 A Verizon Connect customer: “You’re locked into a 3 year agreement with no option for cancellation, other than buyout of the entire contract.”16 Another Verizon customer cancelled in advance and was renewed anyway, then billed:

“They auto renewed it without my consent. Now they are telling me that they have no record of me calling to cancel… Now they want to charge me $1,200 for early termination fee.”17 Persona: Small business owner. Stage: Use and Succeed.

“I just want it to track my van”: the simple job underneath the platform

The job most customers hire for is small and concrete. They want to know where the truck is and prove it later. A pest control manager named his favorite feature as the route record:

“My favorite feature is the bread crumbs. This help when customers say we never showed up. It’s nice to have that record.”18 Persona: Pest control manager. Stage: Use and Succeed.

An industry guide for HVAC and plumbing fleets states the mismatch plainly, that enterprise platforms built for long-haul trucking sell capability the trades will never use:

“If you are an owner-operator running a crew of HVAC techs, plumbers, or electricians, you are likely overpaying for capabilities you will never use.”19 Persona: Owner-operator in the trades. Stage: Research.

When the simple job is done well, the praise is about peace of mind. A plumbing and heating fleet manager: “Azuga allows me to organize my technicians, vehicles and jobs much faster than ever before and gives peace of mind its going smooth.”20 A Verizon Connect dispatcher describes the relief in operational terms:

“Since we set this up, I spend less time chasing drivers for updates. I glance at a map, confirm ETAs, and can reassure nervous shippers without dialing half the fleet.”21 Persona: Transport broker / dispatcher. Stage: Use and Succeed.

“Pings from places I can’t tell”: tracking accuracy and lag

The simple job fails when the data lies. The pin lands in the wrong parking lot, the breadcrumb timestamp is stale, the live map shows a truck where it is not. A warehouse manager cannot use the data he is given:

“There are also pings from places that I cannot tell if the driver stopped there or not.”22 Persona: Fleet/warehouse manager. Stage: Use and Succeed.

A pest control manager watched a truck at the office show as parked somewhere it had left:

“Several times I’ve checked it with a vehicle at the office and it says it hasn’t left an area it was at 30 minutes ago.”23 Persona: Pest control manager. Stage: Use and Succeed.

Accuracy and lag are why customers leave. A compliance manager who switched off Azuga to Samsara named it:

“We switched from Azuga because the platform consistently underperformed. We experienced frequent delays, inaccurate tracking, limited functionality, and poor customer support.”24 Persona: Compliance manager, growing fleet. Stage: Evaluation.

The same complaint lives at every competitor. A Verizon Connect dispatcher: “What bugs me is when the mobile app lags or drops a driver; during the tight pickup window, even the shortest delay makes planning more stressful than it needs to be.”25 A Geotab fleet manager gets false-alarm idle reports at 3 a.m. and thinks a truck is being stolen.26

“Quit penalizing me for braking when a deer ran out”: scoring and alerts that feel unfair

Drivers and the managers who coach them want a score they can defend. The complaint is sensitivity: the system fires on the right reaction. An Azuga driver flags the speeding logic:

“Speeding instructions are not clear, I think if I do 1 or 2Km over speed limit even on highway it would hit my overall score.”27 Persona: Service technician (driver being scored). Stage: Use and Succeed.

The night camera nags. “Distracted driving a little sensitive sometimes when at night and early morning. And don’t like night cam red lights at night.”28 The frustration becomes physical:

“Took some getting used to but doing better it now not cussing at it as much when it goes off.”29 Persona: Older field technician (driver). Stage: Use and Succeed.

The fairness problem is best stated by a Samsara operations manager who can only protect a driver’s score because he has the video proof:

“a harsh brake event because a deer ran out in front of the driver. Harsh braking was the correct action… the videos and data help me fairly dismiss an event when we find something triggered for the ‘right’ reasons, which protects their safety scores.”30 Persona: Operations manager. Stage: Use and Succeed.

This is category-standard pain. A Samsara safety manager describes “excessive alerts that require manual filtering.”31 A Samsara director of safety wants GPS speed used for scoring because “the Samsara speed is often incorrect.”32 When the system gets it right, the driver carries the habit home: “Helps in maintaining my Speed and Braking thus making me a better more careful Driver even in my personal vehicle.”33

“They ghost you”: support that disappears

The fourth theme is support that vanishes when it is needed. Azuga’s offshore service is a repeated sore point. A fleet administrator:

“Dealing with customer service in India has been the most challenging part of my experience. They are nice and say they’ll send a return shipping label & never do without months of multiple follow up emails & calls.”34 Persona: Fleet administrator. Stage: Use and Succeed.

A business assistant who checked the reviews too late turned the company name into an insult:

“it was indeed shortsighted of us not to check the reviews before signing up with Afoolya! Best name for you cause you fooled us into thinking you were a reputable business with some customer service ability.”35 Persona: Business assistant / back office. Stage: Use and Succeed.

A prospect could not even get a demo: “So far their customer service has been awful. I have tried for 2 months to get a demo so this makes me nervous.”36 The word “ghost” is the category’s own. A Geotab safety manager: “a software support that will ghost you when you are having issues.”37 A Verizon Connect owner describes “the run around” from reps in “broken English.”38 Support failure is the most common reason these customers say they left.

2. The Emotional Undercurrent

The reviews carry four named emotions, and they are not the same for the person who signs and the person who drives.

Resignation runs through the customers who stay because leaving costs more than staying. A fleet administrator names the trade plainly:

“The price was lower than competition. That is the only reason. And that is what keeps me staying with Azuga & the issues I have to deal with.”39 Persona: Fleet administrator. Stage: Purchase.

Betrayal is the register at the exit. The language turns moral: “deceived,”40 “LIERS AND BULLYS,”41 a vendor using “corporate power to abuse a customer.”42 A Motive customer reaches for the same word against sales: “the sales people deliberately lie on the phone about the contract terms.”43

Anxiety belongs to the driver. The night camera, the sensitive brake alert, the speeding score that dings a highway crawl. The driver who is “cussing at it when it goes off”44 is not angry at the company; he is anxious about being judged unfairly by a machine.

Relief and quiet pride appear when the simple job lands. The dispatcher who stopped “chasing drivers for updates”45 and the technician who became “a better more careful Driver even in my personal vehicle”46 are the harvestable emotions, the ones the brand should want to reproduce.

The buyer wants out; the driver wants fair: an explicit split

The economic buyer and the driver feel opposite things about the same product, and they want opposite reassurances. This split should drive how the messaging is segmented.

The buyer, the owner or fleet administrator who signs, fears the contract. Their loudest emotion is the dread of the exit bill and the betrayal of the fine print: “stuck in a long-term contract that I actively tried to prevent,”47 billed “$4,500” with no explanation.48 What calms the buyer is a clean, short, exit-friendly term.

The driver, the technician being scored, fears the judgment. Their emotion is the anxiety of being penalized for a correct reaction, of “braking when a deer ran out”49 or doing “1 or 2Km over speed limit even on highway.”50 What calms the driver is fair scoring they can contest with evidence, the way the Samsara manager dismisses a harsh-brake event with video.51

Sell freedom to the buyer. Sell fairness to the driver. Messaging that promises both, to the right person, closes the split.

3. Why They Hired

The hire timeline runs from a first nagging thought to a settled habit. The public record is strongest at the ends, the first thought and the eventual habit, and thinnest in the middle, which is what the recommended primary interviews would fill.

First thought and passive looking. The trigger is a failure of the old tool, usually accuracy or support. A construction manager left Verizon Connect:

“The product was not reliable and the customer service was terrible.”52 Persona: Construction fleet manager. Stage: Evaluation.

A facilities director left Geotab over hardware and install support:

“Hard to dig for data, geotabs did not always work, customer service was poor, not technical in install support.”53 Persona: Director of facilities. Stage: Evaluation.
  1. Active evaluation. Buyers shop on price and on getting a straight answer. A prospect shopping to leave Verizon Connect tried for two months to get a demo and grew “nervous.”54 The price draw is explicit: customers describe Azuga as “a cheap, effective alternative to the higher priced options.”55
  2. Decision. Price closes the deal. The fleet administrator who stays says it outright: “The price was lower than competition. That is the only reason.”56 A flagship customer frames price and service together: “You set the gold standard with price and service.”57
  3. First use and habit. The habit forms around the daily glance at the map and the breadcrumb record that wins arguments with customers.58 The dispatcher who no longer dials “half the fleet”59 and the technician carrying safer driving into his personal vehicle60 are at the habit stage, where the product has become part of the workday.

4. Why They Almost Didn’t, and Why Some Left

The objections cluster on the forces that push a buyer toward change, pull them to a new tool, raise anxiety about switching, and anchor them in old habits.

  • Push, the pain that starts the search. Unreliable hardware and absent support. The Azuga 2.0 redesign is its own push for existing users:
“Azuga 2.0 is horrible compared to the original fleet management they originally had. A lot of really cool features are no longer a thing.”61 Persona: Existing Azuga GPS user (service trades). Stage: Use and Succeed.
  • Pull, what draws them in. Cleaner interface and the simple tracking job done right. A service-trades user names both the pull and the friction in one breath:
“I have found the UI to be cleaner, but there are a few features that I miss about the old UI.”62 Persona: Service-trades Azuga GPS user. Stage: Use and Succeed.
  • Anxiety, the fear of the new choice. The three-year contract is the biggest anxiety, and it kills deals at the door. The landscaping operator who found the 3-year term “alarming” also wanted a trial and was refused:
“We also tried to get a test unit to see how everything worked and they said they do not do that anymore so that was another turn off.”63 Persona: Service-trades operator evaluating Azuga. Stage: Evaluation.
  • Broken promises, the deepest objection. What was sold did not match what was delivered. A buyer ordered live-stream cameras that could not live stream and was billed for the gap:
“never told me they would charge us an additional $14,000 for unused software features (virtually nothing!)”64 Persona: Fleet buyer who wanted live camera view. Stage: Use and Succeed.
“there were also blatant discrepancies between what was promised in our contract and what was delivered. Their lack of transparency and failure to uphold their end of the agreement was disheartening.”65 Persona: Admin manager. Stage: Use and Succeed.

5. The Language Gap

Customers and vendors describe the same product in different words. The vendor column below holds the category cliche every telematics seller reaches for, drawn from no single company’s copy. The shift column is the plain language to adopt.

What customers say What vendors say (category cliche) The shift to make
“I just want it to track my van”66 comprehensive safety platform Lead with the simple job: see your trucks, prove the route.
“they will automatically screw you”67 auto-renewal / term agreement Promise the exit in plain words: leave when you want, no buyout.
“they want to charge me all future income”68 early termination fee State the cancellation cost up front, in dollars, before signing.
“quit penalizing me for braking when a deer ran out”69 driver coaching / safety score Promise contestable scoring: dispute a bad flag with the video.
“they ghost you / liers and bullys”70 customer success Promise a named human who answers, not a queue.
“it goes off and I’m cussing at it”71 event / alert notification Tune for the real reaction; fewer, fairer alerts.
“proof when customers say we never showed up”72 breadcrumb trail / route history Sell the proof, not the breadcrumb: win the no-show dispute.

Words to adopt: track my van, prove I showed up, leave when you want, no buyout, fair scoring, dispute the flag, a person who answers, peace of mind.

Words to drop: comprehensive, platform, solution, term agreement, auto-renewal, early termination fee, customer success, coaching, event, robust.

6. What They Feel and Do Not Say

Some pains sit under the quotes, supported by the pattern across them.

They blame themselves for not reading the reviews first. The “Afoolya” reviewer says it directly, that it was “shortsighted of us not to check the reviews before signing up.”73 Under the anger is a buyer who feels foolish, and who will warn the next buyer to do what they failed to do. The 60-day “BUYER BEWARE”74 post is that warning made public.

They expect the trial Azuga removed. The landscaping operator named the refused test unit as “another turn off.”75 Behind that is an unstated standard: in this category, buyers who feel burned by long contracts now expect to try before they commit, and removing the trial reads as something to hide.

  • Drivers feel surveilled, not coached, even when they accept it. The technician “cussing at it”76 and the one who dislikes “night cam red lights”77 rarely say “I feel watched.” The accumulation of sensitivity complaints is that feeling. The driver who became safer at home78 shows the inverse, that the same tool feels like coaching when the scoring is fair.

Price-led loyalty is fragile. The administrator who stays “only” for price79 is held in place by the contract term. The unspoken message is that the day a cheaper or cleaner option appears, she is gone, and the term is the only thing keeping her.

7. Rave Notes: a Testimonial Bank

A harvest a copywriter can pull from directly. Every quote is real and cited.

On price and value

“You set the gold standard with price and service.”80 John Phillips, Service Manager, Acree Plumbing & Air.
“Far and away I get everything I need for the best price out there.”81 David McCauley, North American Fleet Manager, Service Experts.
“every month or so, Azuga comes out with innovative features that make their platform an even better bang for the buck.”82 Patty Middleton, Safety Director, California UMI.

On ease of use

“It is the easiest, most intuitive system I have used to date. Everything that I need is a click away.”83 Patty Middleton, Safety Director, California UMI.
“I have found the UI to be cleaner.”84 Service-trades Azuga GPS user, Aspire forum.

On visibility and peace of mind

“Azuga allows me to organize my technicians, vehicles and jobs much faster than ever before and gives peace of mind its going smooth.”85 Connors Plumbing and Heating Fleet Manager.
“You can pull up that map, know where all your assets are, if technicians have completed the job, standing still for 3 hours or if they haven’t gotten there yet.”86 Bryan Dardar, VP of Stationary Services, Nolan Power Group.
“Azuga helps me understand where my guys are and to check that the job is getting done and that the team is doing what they are supposed to be doing.”87 Tommy Kellogg, Co-Founder, Environmental Pest Control.

On the breadcrumb proof

“My favorite feature is the bread crumbs. This help when customers say we never showed up. It’s nice to have that record.”88 Pest control manager, Capterra.

On safer driving

“Helps in maintaining my Speed and Braking thus making me a better more careful Driver even in my personal vehicle.”89 Senior service technician, Capterra.
“Azuga has been everything and more than expected, creating a highly engaged workforce, empowering each employee to modify his/her driving behavior in real-time.”90 Chris McKeeman, VP, EHS, Numotion.

On switching to Azuga

“We recently switched to Azuga from a hard-install GPS provider and are delighted at the results.”91 Remy Cano, President, Northwest Tank.

8. Strategic Implications

Each move below translates the customer voice into action and reinforces a recommendation already on the table. The purpose is to close the gap between how the market perceives Azuga and the position leadership has chosen, not to remake Azuga into a copy of what reviewers ask for.

Commission a primary-interview and case-study program first. Every quote here is a public review, and no Azuga customer was interviewed. The emotional registers, the buyer-versus-driver split, the hire timeline, and the win and loss forces are inferred from reviews and need confirmation from live conversation. Run an AI-led custom-interview pass with current service-trade customers before the messaging hardens, and convert the strongest sessions into published, named case studies. Commission it.

  • Make the no-traps promise true, then headline it. Contract entrapment sits in roughly two-thirds of Azuga’s negative reviews,92 the same trap customers fled Samsara, Motive, and Verizon Connect to escape. This is the contract and cancellation recommendation in the Brief. Convert the standard term to genuinely flexible, no-auto-renewal terms with a capped, plainly stated exit, fix the billing-after-cancellation failures, then turn “leave when you want” into the headline counter-position against rival lock-in. Deploy it.
  • Lead with the simple job and the dollar outcome, not the platform. Customers hire to “track my van” and prove the route, and the flagship cases pay off in named dollars: Acree cut premiums by $80,000 a year, California UMI took a 12% fleet-wide insurance discount.9394 This supports the “safety that pays” pillar. Front the named savings with the simple-job promise underneath, and demote “comprehensive safety platform.” Anchor it.
  1. Ungate human support and out-deliver before claiming it. The “ghost you” and offshore complaints959697 undercut any service claim Azuga makes today. This is the service recommendation in the Brief. Put a named human point of contact on every tier and fix response time first, then claim “a person who answers, not a queue.” Sequence it behind the fix, then ship it.
  2. Give drivers a way to contest a bad flag. The sensitivity complaints9899100 map to a fairness gap the buyer-versus-driver split makes a messaging segment. Let drivers and coaches dispute a flag with the video, the way the Samsara manager dismisses the deer-brake event,101 and message fairness to the driver while messaging freedom to the buyer. Build the dispute path, then promote it.
  3. Reviews are the new sales floor. Prospects are reading public reviews mid-evaluation and walking before they ever reach a rep: the Aspire operator who called the 3-year term “alarming”102 and the “Afoolya” reviewer who wished she had checked BBB first103 are deciding inside the review surface, well before the sales call. The review pages are a sales channel Azuga does not control today. Stand up a managed review-response and review-generation program on Capterra, Trustpilot, BBB, and the trade forums, respond to every contract complaint with the new exit terms, and seed the rave notes above from satisfied service-trade customers. Own it.

Notes

  1. Capterra, Azuga Fleet, Steve P (Service pest tech, Chemicals), Sep 16 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/153228/Azuga-Fleet/reviews/
  2. Capterra, Azuga Fleet, Jason L (Pest Control Manager, Public Safety), Sep 17 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/153228/Azuga-Fleet/reviews/

  21. Capterra, Verizon Connect, John P (Transport Broker, Transportation/Trucking/Railroad), Dec 5 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/72551/Fleetmatics-Work/reviews/
  22. Capterra, Azuga Fleet, Jeff P (Warehouse manager, Chemicals), Sep 16 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/153228/Azuga-Fleet/reviews/
  23. Capterra, Azuga Fleet, Jason L (Pest Control Manager, Public Safety), Sep 17 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/153228/Azuga-Fleet/reviews/

  25. Capterra, Verizon Connect, John P (Transport Broker, Transportation/Trucking/Railroad), Dec 5 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/72551/Fleetmatics-Work/reviews/

  27. Capterra, Azuga Fleet, Prem G (Service Pro, Consumer Services), Sep 17 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/153228/Azuga-Fleet/reviews/
  28. Capterra, Azuga Fleet, Kerry C (Technician, Consumer Services), Sep 16 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/153228/Azuga-Fleet/reviews/
  29. Capterra, Azuga Fleet, Kerry C (Technician, Consumer Services), Sep 16 2025, https://www.capterra.com/p/153228/Azuga-Fleet/reviews/
